description: User-controlled req.query.q is only length-limited, not escaped or parameterized, then directly string-concatenated into a raw SQL query executed via sequelize.query, allowing SQL injection (e.g. via quote characters in the search term).

`criteria` is derived directly from `req.query.q` (only truncated to 200 chars, never escaped or parameterized) and is concatenated directly into the raw SQL string passed to `sequelize.query`, allowing arbitrary SQL injection via the `q` query parameter. This is OWASP Juice Shop's intentionally vulnerable dbSchemaChallenge code demonstrating classic string-concatenation SQL injection.

criteria is taken directly from req.query.q and concatenated into a raw SQL string via template literal in models.sequelize.query, with only a denylist regex (quotes, ';', 'and'/'or', '#') as a defense rather than parameterized queries/bind params — denylist filters are commonly bypassable (encoding tricks, alternate injection vectors not covered by the list) so this is not equivalent to a safe parameterized query.

User-controlled req.query.q is concatenated directly into a raw SQL string passed to sequelize.query; the only defense is a handwritten blocklist regex ('"|;|and|or|#) which is a well-known insufficient sanitization pattern (misses keywords like UNION/SELECT, backslash escaping, encoding tricks, and case/whitespace variants) rather than parameterized queries/bind params, so injection bypasses are plausible.

The code manually escapes only `<` and `>` via replaceAll, but omits `&`, `"`, and `'`, which is an incomplete/bypassable HTML-escaping approach compared to a proper sanitizer — exactly the weak-sanitization pattern this rule targets. Note this is inside Juice Shop's `codefixes` directory, which holds intentionally-flawed candidate fixes for a training challenge, so it may be a deliberately incorrect answer option rather than shipped production code.

The code performs manual blocklist-based HTML escaping via replaceAll for only '<' and '>', omitting '&', quotes, and other characters needed for context-aware escaping, so it can be bypassed (e.g., attribute-context injection or double-encoding issues) — exactly the fragile pattern the rule targets. Without seeing a proper allowlist-based sanitizer or confirmation the output is never rendered as raw HTML/attribute, this is a genuine instance of the flagged anti-pattern rather than a mischaracte

User-controlled `criteria` from req.query.q is concatenated directly into a raw SQL string via template literal; the only attempted mitigation (`criteria.replace(...)`) discards its return value without reassigning, so it has no effect, leaving classic string-concatenation SQL injection reachable from untrusted input.

criteria is derived directly from req.query.q and interpolated via template literal into a raw sequelize.query string; the only sanitization attempt on line 5 (criteria.replace(...)) discards its return value instead of reassigning to criteria, so it has zero effect, leaving unescaped user input directly in the SQL statement.

The guard on line 6 uses `!startsWith("apple") || !startsWith("orange")`, which by De Morgan's law is true for every possible string (no string can start with both prefixes simultaneously), so the function always returns 400 before reaching the query on line 10 — the interpolated query is dead code, unreachable from any input. This is also a static Juice Shop 'codefixes' challenge-answer snippet (one of several candidate fixes shown to the player), not live production code path.

This file lives under juice-shop/data/static/codefixes/, which are static quiz-answer snippets shown to users to pick the correct fix — they are not executed as part of the running server, so the query is never actually invoked with live input. Even taken as real code, the buggy guard (`||` instead of `&&`) makes the condition always true, so it always returns 400 before reaching the query, making line 10 unreachable regardless.

This is a hardcoded example JWT (the well-known jwt.io sample token) used in a unit test spec file to verify the tokenDecode() method's parsing logic; it is not a real credential and poses no security risk.

This is a hardcoded JWT used as test fixture data in a Jasmine/Vitest spec file (last-login-ip.component.spec.ts) to verify component parsing logic, not a live credential or secret embedded in application/production code; it decodes to trivial mock data ({lastLoginIp: '1.2.3.4'}) and is not reachable as an actual authentication token.

This is a hardcoded JWT token in a Jasmine/Karma test spec file (last-login-ip.component.spec.ts), used purely as a mock localStorage value to test that the component correctly parses an empty JWT payload; it is not a real credential, is not reachable from untrusted input, and poses no exploitable risk.

The loop only reads nested properties (replacementValue = replacementValue[property]) to fetch a value for string interpolation; it never assigns to replacementValue[property], so it cannot write to Object.prototype. The property chain also comes from a hardcoded hacking-instructor tutorial config, not attacker-controlled input.

challengeKey here originates from static filenames of bundled coding-challenge YAML files (derived in getCodeChallenges() from paths under data/static/codeChallenges/), not from user-supplied request input, so an attacker cannot control the interpolated regex fragment; Semgrep's non-literal-regexp rule is flagging pattern shape rather than actual taint from an untrusted source.

challengeKey comes from static internal challenge/yaml metadata defined by the developer (challenge identifiers configured in the codebase), not from untrusted user input, so there is no attacker-controlled path to construct a malicious regex payload here; this is a generic non-literal-regexp lint match rather than an exploitable ReDoS.

An RSA private key is hardcoded directly as a string literal in source code and used for JWT signing, matching the rule's detection intent exactly; regardless of application context, embedding a private key in source is a genuine secret-management defect.

The HMAC key is a hardcoded string literal directly in source code used for cryptographic signing (crypto.createHmac), which is a genuine hardcoded-secret defect regardless of this being intentionally-vulnerable demo code (OWASP Juice Shop); anyone with the source can forge HMACs.

Line 54 signs the JWT with the variable `privateKey`, not an inline literal secret; the actual hardcoded credential in this file is the HMAC key on line 42, which is a different line/pattern. Without seeing privateKey's definition (in Juice Shop it's loaded from an RSA key file, not hardcoded here), this specific line does not itself contain a hardcoded credential.

The HMAC key used to sign the deluxeToken is a module-level constant (privateKey) rather than being loaded from an environment variable or secrets store, and this token is used for security-relevant role verification elsewhere in the file, making a hardcoded key a genuine CWE-798 defect assuming privateKey is a literal/constant as implied by the code shown.

res.cookie('token', token) is called with no options object, so it uses Express defaults which omit HttpOnly, Secure, and SameSite, leaving the auth token cookie exposed to client-side script access and cross-site transmission.

The token value is not arbitrary attacker-controlled input passed straight into res.cookie — it is only re-set after jwt.verify() succeeds against the server's public key, meaning it must be a validly-signed JWT issued by the app's own auth flow rather than an attacker-forged session identifier. This is a same-value cookie refresh on an already-authenticated token, not the classic pre-auth session ID being carried across a privilege boundary, so the pattern-match on req flowing into res.cookie i

The dialect is 'sqlite', so the Sequelize username/password arguments are ignored entirely by the driver; the literal strings 'username'/'password' are placeholder values, not real credentials for any live service.

User-controlled `body.orderLinesData` flows directly into `vm.runInContext('safeEval(orderLinesData)', ...)` with no validation or sanitization, and Node's `vm` module is well known to be escapable, making this a genuine eval-injection/RCE sink reachable from untrusted request input (this is Juice Shop's intentional RCE challenge).

The string passed to eval() is built entirely from server-generated random numbers and a fixed operator list (['*','+','-']); no part of the expression derives from user/request input, so there is no injection vector despite the eval() usage.

The `fields` query parameter is attacker-controlled and used directly as a property key to read arbitrary properties off `user.data` and copy them into the response, bypassing the intended allowlist (id/email/lastLoginIp/profileImage) enforced in the else branch — e.g. a request with `?fields=password` would leak the password hash or other sensitive fields that were deliberately excluded from the default response.

updatedEmail is derived via `email.replace(/[aeiou]/gi,'*')`, which always yields a plain string, so it cannot carry MongoDB operator objects (e.g. `$ne`, `$gt`) needed for NoSQL injection; the query `{ email: updatedEmail }` is a simple string equality match. Additionally the value originates from the authenticated user's own session data (loggedInUser.data.email), not directly from req.body/query.

The `email` value passed into the MongoDB query is derived from request data without any type/sanitization check shown in this snippet, so if it originates from req.body as in Juice Shop's actual implementation, an attacker can submit an object (e.g. {$ne: null}) instead of a string to inject MongoDB query operators and bypass the intended filter.

The allowlist only checks the file extension/name suffix (endsWithAllowlistedFileType) and strips a poison null byte, but never canonicalizes or restricts the resolved path to the 'ftp/' directory, so a filename containing '../' sequences (with an allowed suffix) passed to path.resolve('ftp/', file) can escape the intended directory before being served via res.sendFile. This is in fact one of OWASP Juice Shop's intentionally vulnerable, documented directory-traversal challenges in this exact fil

The handler rejects any `file` param containing '/', so `path.resolve('encryptionkeys/', file)` can never be given a multi-segment traversal payload; a bare '..' (no slash) only resolves one level up to a directory, which sendFile cannot serve as a file, so no arbitrary file read is achievable through this input.

The `id` value comes directly from `req.body.id` with no type validation or sanitization and is passed straight into `findOne({ _id: id })` (and later `update`), so a JSON body where `id` is an object (e.g. `{"$ne": null}` or `{"$gt": ""}`) lets an attacker inject MongoDB query operators, a classic NoSQL injection pattern intentionally present in Juice Shop.

The `id` value used directly as the MongoDB `_id` filter at line 36 (and line 25) originates from the request body/params with no type validation or sanitization shown, allowing an attacker to pass an object like `{"$ne": null}` to manipulate the query — this is the known NoSQL injection pattern intentionally present in Juice Shop's likeProductReviews route.

This is OWASP Juice Shop's deliberately vulnerable likeProductReviews route, where the `id` used in `_id: id` for both the update and findOne queries is taken directly from unsanitized request body/params (req.body.id in the actual source) without validation, allowing NoSQL operator injection (e.g. passing `{"$ne": null}`) to match arbitrary review documents. This is an intentional seeded vulnerability, not a false trigger.

The `id` used in both the findOne (line 43) and update (line 51) queries is passed directly into MongoDB filter objects without type-checking or sanitization; semgrep's taint trace confirms it originates from a `req` argument (this is OWASP Juice Shop's intentionally vulnerable 'forged review' NoSQL injection challenge, where a crafted `id` such as an object with `$ne`/`$gt` operators bypasses the intended lookup and lets an attacker manipulate arbitrary reviews).

The blacklist only rejects filenames containing '/', but a value like '..' contains no slash and still causes path.resolve('logs/', '..') to escape the logs directory to its parent, and there is no canonicalization/containment check against the intended base directory afterward, allowing arbitrary file read outside logs/ (this is the known intentionally-vulnerable Juice Shop path-traversal challenge).

req.body.email is directly interpolated into a raw SQL string passed to sequelize.query with no parameterization or escaping, allowing classic SQL injection (e.g., ' OR 1=1--) — this is OWASP Juice Shop's intentional login bypass vulnerability.

req.body.email and req.body.password are directly string-interpolated into a raw SQL query passed to sequelize.query without parameterization, allowing an attacker to break out of the string literals and inject SQL (e.g., classic ' OR 1=1-- authentication bypass).

The email value comes from the authenticated session lookup (loggedInUser.data.email), not directly from req.body/query/params, and is further transformed via a fixed regex replace into a plain string before being placed as the value of a fixed 'email' key — there is no way for a client to inject a MongoDB operator object here.

req.params.id is an Express route parameter, which is always parsed as a plain string (unlike req.query/req.body, which can be crafted into nested objects like {$ne:1}); used as a scalar equality match on _id it cannot carry Mongo operators, so no injection payload is deliverable through this parameter.

The handler only blocks a literal '/' character before passing user-controlled `file` into path.resolve and res.sendFile, but this does not canonicalize or restrict the resolved path to the quarantine directory — traversal via backslashes (Windows), encoded separators, or other non-'/' techniques can still escape the intended directory. This is Juice Shop's intentionally vulnerable file-serving endpoint, a known path traversal challenge.

This is OWASP Juice Shop's known open-redirect challenge: res.redirect(toUrl) redirects to user-controlled query.to, gated only by isRedirectAllowed's allowlist check which Juice Shop intentionally implements with a bypassable substring/startsWith match, making the redirect target attacker-controllable despite the nominal check.

query.to is untrusted user input passed to res.redirect(); while isRedirectAllowed() gates the call, its implementation isn't shown here, and the adjacent isUnintendedRedirect helper in the same file uses a naive startsWith prefix-match against an allowlist, a pattern known to be bypassable for open redirects — this is Juice Shop's intentionally vulnerable redirect challenge.

This is OWASP Juice Shop's redirect handler, which gates res.redirect on security.isRedirectAllowed(toUrl), but the presence of a dedicated 'redirectChallenge'/isUnintendedRedirect() check confirms the app intentionally ships a bypassable allowlist so users can complete an open-redirect CTF challenge. Without seeing isRedirectAllowed's implementation we can't rule out that it's a solid check, but given known Juice Shop design this is a genuine, intended open-redirect vulnerability rather than a

User-controlled req.query.q is directly interpolated into a raw SQL string via template literals and passed to sequelize.query with no parameterization or escaping, only a length truncation which does not prevent injection; this is OWASP Juice Shop's intentional SQL injection challenge.

User-supplied req.query.q is truncated but not escaped or parameterized and is directly interpolated into a raw SQL string passed to sequelize.query, allowing SQL injection; this is OWASP Juice Shop's intentional unionSqlInjectionChallenge.

User-controlled req.params.id is concatenated directly into a MongoDB $where clause, which evaluates arbitrary JavaScript server-side; when the noSqlCommandChallenge path is active the value is only truncated (not type-coerced), so injection payloads reach the query unsanitized — this is Juice Shop's intentionally vulnerable NoSQL command injection challenge.

When the reflectedXssChallenge flag is enabled, `id` is only length-truncated (utils.trunc), not character-sanitized, and is interpolated directly into a `$where` JavaScript string executed by MongoDB, allowing NoSQL/JS injection via req.params.id; this is Juice Shop's intentional noSqlOrdersChallenge vulnerability.

req.body.id is passed directly and unsanitized into the MongoDB query filter, allowing operator injection (e.g. {"$ne": null}) to bypass the intended single-document match; this is Juice Shop's intentional noSqlReviewsChallenge vulnerability.

res.cookie('token', updatedToken) sets an authentication token cookie with no options object, so it lacks HttpOnly, Secure, and SameSite attributes, making it readable via JS (XSS) and eligible for cross-site submission (CSRF); no sanitization or later cookie config is shown to mitigate this.

The cookie value `updatedToken` is a freshly generated auth token from `security.authorize(userWithStatus)`, not a raw attacker-supplied value; the taint flagged is a derived/re-signed token, not something the user can set directly, so this isn't a genuine session-fixation sink.

username is user-controlled (set via registration/profile) and a substring of it is passed directly to eval() when a regex pattern matches, allowing arbitrary JavaScript execution from attacker-supplied data; this is OWASP Juice Shop's intentional SSTI/code-injection challenge.

The username field, which is user-controllable via registration/profile update, is regex-matched for an SSTI pattern and the extracted substring is passed directly to eval() with no sanitization, allowing arbitrary JavaScript execution in the application process.

User-controlled username is substring-extracted and passed directly to eval() with no sanitization, only gated by a regex that fails open (falls through to eval whenever it matches), making it a genuine code injection sink reachable from untrusted profile input.

Line 57 only calls utils.contains(subs, ...) to detect whether a specific XSS payload string is present in subs, for challenge-solved tracking (this is OWASP Juice Shop, an intentionally vulnerable training app) — it does not write subs into the HTML/script output. No rendering sink is shown at this line, so the flagged line itself is not an XSS injection point.

The code encodes other template values (e.g., `entities.encode(config.get('application.name'))` at line 61) before insertion, but `subs` is concatenated raw into a `<script>` tag with no escaping, and its origin is not visible/controlled at this point in the code — matching the known OWASP Juice Shop pattern where subtitle file content is embedded unsanitized, a genuine missing-sanitization XSS sink.

The `name` argument is always a hardcoded string literal passed at call sites within this same file (e.g. 'validatePreconditions', 'cleanupFtpFolder'), never derived from user input, and `err` is passed as a separate argument to console.error rather than interpolated into a format string, so there is no attacker-controlled format specifier injection possible.

The code explicitly mounts `serveIndex('ftp', { icons: true })` on the public `/ftp` route, enabling directory listing/browsing of the ftp folder's contents to any client; this is confirmed by the surrounding comments identifying it as the intentional 'directoryListingChallenge' in Juice Shop, so the rule correctly flags a real (deliberately vulnerable) directory-listing exposure reachable from untrusted requests.

The code explicitly mounts serveIndex on '/.well-known' with directory listing enabled ({icons: true, view: 'details'}), which will render a browsable directory index of that folder's contents to any client; this is a direct, unconditional instance of the directory-listing pattern the rule targets, not sanitized or gated by auth.

serveIndex is mounted directly on '/encryptionkeys' with no auth/access-control middleware in front of it (unlike the '/support/logs' route below which has verify.accessControlChallenges()), so directory listing is genuinely enabled and exposed; this is intentionally-vulnerable OWASP Juice Shop challenge code, but the reported defect class (directory listing exposure) is accurately identified.

The code explicitly mounts serveIndex('logs', {view:'details'}) on /support/logs, enabling directory listing that exposes filenames/details of files in the logs directory to any client requesting that path; no code shown restricts access before the listing is rendered.
